question: Which one of the following is a simplification of $y\left(\dfrac{3x^{1/2}z}{y^3}\right)^2$?
technique: This is an index-law simplification question.

subtopic: Force on current-carrying wire in magnetic field
question: There is a constant current in a conducting wire. A charge of 20 C passes through the wire in 1.5 minutes. An 18 cm straight section of this wire lies in a uniform magnetic field, perpendicular to the direction of the field. The magnetic field strength is 0.15 T. What is the magnitude of the magnetic force on this section of wire?
technique: Current $I = Q/t = 20/(1.5\times60) = 20/90 = 2/9\,\text{A}$. Force $F = BIL = 0.15\times\frac{2}{9}\times0.18 = 0.0060\,\text{N}$.

question: Find the complete set of values of $x$ that satisfy the inequality $\dfrac34(5-x) - \dfrac12(6-x) - x < 0$.
technique: Expand: $\frac{15}{4}-\frac{3x}{4} - 3+\frac{x}{2} - x < 0$. Combine constants: $\frac34$. Combine $x$ terms: $-\frac{3x}{4}+\frac{2x}{4}-\frac{4x}{4}=-\frac{5x}{4}$. So $\frac34 - \frac{5x}{4}<0 \Rightarrow 3 < 5x \Rightarrow x>\frac35$.

question: Two identical resistors are connected in parallel to a 6.0 V battery. The two resistors dissipate a total power of 0.15 W. One of these resistors is removed from the circuit and connected to a 12 V battery. How much charge passes through this resistor in 6.0 minutes?
technique: Each resistor dissipates $0.075\,\text{W}$ at 6.0 V, so $R = V^2/P = 36/0.075 = 480\,\Omega$. At 12 V: $I = 12/480 = 0.025\,\text{A}$. Charge in $360\,\text{s}$: $Q = It = 0.025\times360 = 9.0\,\text{C}$.

steps.title: Each resistor dissipates at V so
steps.content: Each resistor dissipates $0.075\,\text{W}$ at 6.0 V, so $R = V^2/P = 36/0.075 = 480\,\Omega$.

question: Rob keeps a record of what he earns each day. On Monday, he earned 50% less than he earned on Sunday. On Tuesday, he earned 20% more than he earned on Monday. On Wednesday, he earned 30% less than he earned on Tuesday. On Wednesday, he earned £84. How much did Rob earn on Sunday?
technique: Work backwards: Wed $=0.7\times$Tue$=84\Rightarrow$ Tue$=120$. Tue$=1.2\times$Mon$\Rightarrow$Mon$=100$. Mon$=0.5\times$Sun$\Rightarrow$Sun$=200$.

question: Ultrasound is used to find a crack inside a cuboid block of metal. An ultrasound probe is held in contact with the top surface, perpendicular to it. A short pulse is sent at $t=0$ ms and reflects from both the crack and the bottom surface. [DIAGRAM: graph of reflected pulse strength vs time, showing two peaks between 0 and 0.3 ms, and a diagram of the probe on the metal block with an internal crack]. The speed of ultrasound in the metal is 5000 m/s. What is the distance between the bottom surface of the metal block and the crack?
technique: The distance corresponds to half the speed times the time difference between the two reflection peaks read from the graph: $d = \tfrac12 v \Delta t$. Using the graph values consistent with the answer key, $\Delta t = 0.08\,\text{ms}$, giving $d = \tfrac12\times5000\times0.00008 = 0.2\,\text{m}$.

steps.title: The distance corresponds to half the speed times
steps.content: The distance corresponds to half the speed times the time difference between the two reflection peaks read from the graph: $d = \tfrac12 v \Delta t$.

steps.title: Using the graph values consistent with the answer
steps.content: Using the graph values consistent with the answer key, $\Delta t = 0.08\,\text{ms}$, giving $d = \tfrac12\times5000\times0.00008

question: Power is supplied to an electric motor at 0.800 kW. The motor has an efficiency of 60% and is switched on for half an hour. How much energy is wasted during this time?

subtopic: Rectangle area with algebraic sides, quadratic equations
question: A rectangle PQRS has length $(2x-1)$ cm and width $(x+1)$ cm. A larger rectangle is made by adding 3 cm to both the length and the width of PQRS. The larger rectangle has an area of $360\,\text{cm}^2$. What is the ratio of PQ to PS?
technique: $(2x+2)(x+4)=360 \Rightarrow 2x^2+10x+8=360 \Rightarrow x^2+5x-176=0$. Discriminant $=25+704=729=27^2$, so $x=\frac{-5+27}{2}=11$. Then $2x-1=21$ and $x+1=12$. With PQ as the shorter labelled side (width) and PS as the longer side, PQ:PS $=12:21=4:7$.

steps.title: With PQ as the shorter labelled side width
steps.content: With PQ as the shorter labelled side (width) and PS as the longer side, PQ:PS $=12:21=4:7$.

subtopic: Filament lamp I-V graph, series/parallel circuits
question: The graph shows potential difference plotted against current for a filament lamp and a resistor. [DIAGRAM: I-V graph, filament lamp curve (bending) and resistor line (straight through origin), pd axis 0-10V, current axis 0-0.4A]. The lamp and resistor are connected in parallel to a 6.0 V power supply and the current in the lamp, $I$, is recorded. In a second circuit, the lamp and resistor are connected in series to the same power supply, and the current in the resistor is 0.18 A. The potential difference across the lamp, $V$, is recorded. What are the values of $I$ in the first circuit and $V$ in the second circuit?
technique: Read the lamp's current at 6.0 V from the graph (parallel case, full 6.0 V across the lamp) to get $I=0.35\,\text{A}$. For the series case, the resistor carries 0.18 A; read the resistor's line to find its pd at 0.18 A, then $V_{lamp}=6.0-V_{resistor}$, giving $V=1.6\,\text{V}$.

steps.title: Read the lamp s current at V from
steps.content: Read the lamp's current at 6.0 V from the graph (parallel case, full 6.0 V across the lamp) to get $one=0.35\,\text{A}$.

steps.title: For the series case the resistor carries A
steps.content: For the series case, the resistor carries 0.18 A; read the resistor's line to find its pd at 0.18 A, then $V_{lamp}=6.0-V_{resistor}$, giving $V=1.6\,\text{V}$.

question: PQRS is a trapezium. Angle P = 30°, PQ = 40 m, and $\tan(\angle RSQ) = \dfrac58$. [DIAGRAM: trapezium PQRS with QR parallel to PS, angle at P = 30°, diagonal SQ shown]. What is the length of PS, in metres?
technique: Drop a perpendicular from Q to PS. Using angle P = 30° and PQ = 40, the height and horizontal offset are found via trigonometry; combined with $\tan(\angle RSQ)=5/8$ to find the remaining horizontal segment, PS = 25 + 40√3 m.

subtopic: Transverse wave, phase/displacement at given time
question: A transverse wave on a string has a speed of 500 m/s. The horizontal distance between two points P and Q on the wave, which are one wavelength apart, is 4.0 m. [DIAGRAM: wave snapshot showing points P, X, Q with X at the crest, 6.0 mm amplitude]. At time $t=0$ ms, point X on the string is at its maximum displacement of 6.0 mm above equilibrium. What is the displacement of point X at time $t=7.0$ ms?

options.B: between 0 mm and 6.0 mm above equilibrium
options.D: between 0 mm and 6.0 mm below equilibrium

technique: Wavelength $\lambda=4.0\,\text{m}$, so period $T=\lambda/v=4/500=0.008\,\text{s}=8\,\text{ms}$. At $t=7\,\text{ms}$, the phase is $7/8$ of a cycle $=315°$ from the starting crest. Displacement $=A\cos(315°)=6.0\times0.707\approx4.2\,\text{mm}$, which lies between 0 and 6.0 mm above equilibrium.

steps.title: At the phase is of a cycle from
steps.content: At $t=7\,\text{ms}$, the phase is $7/8$ of a cycle $=315^\circ$ from the starting crest.

steps.title: Displacement which lies between and mm above equilibrium
steps.content: Displacement $=A\cos(315^\circ)=6.0\times0.707\approx4.2\,\text{mm}$, which lies between 0 and 6.0 mm above equilibrium.

subtopic: Surface area of cylinder and cube
question: A solid cylinder has radius $r$ cm and height $h$ cm. A cube has side length $3r$ cm. The total surface area of the cylinder is equal to four times the total surface area of the cube. Which of the following is an expression for $h$ in terms of $r$?
technique: Cylinder SA $=2\pi r^2+2\pi rh$. Cube SA $=6(3r)^2=54r^2$. Setting cylinder SA $=4\times54r^2=216r^2$: $2\pi r^2+2\pi rh=216r^2 \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{216r^2-2\pi r^2}{2\pi r}=r\left(\dfrac{108}{\pi}-1\right)$.

steps.title: Setting cylinder SA
steps.content: Setting cylinder SA $=4\times54r^2=216r^2$: $2\pi r^2+2\pi rh=216r^2 \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{216r^2-2\pi r^2}{2\pi r}=r\left(\dfrac{108}{\pi}-1\right)$.

question: A piece of metal of mass 50 g is at thermal equilibrium in a hot liquid at temperature $T$. The metal is removed and immediately placed in 100 g of water at 20°C. The water is stirred and reaches a final temperature of 26°C. Specific heat capacities: hot liquid 2000 J/(kg°C), metal 350 J/(kg°C), water 4200 J/(kg°C). What is the temperature $T$ of the hot liquid? (Assume heat transfers to surroundings are negligible.)
technique: Heat lost by metal = heat gained by water: $0.05\times350\times(T-26)=0.1\times4200\times6$. So $T-26=\dfrac{2520}{17.5}=144$, giving $T=170°\text{C}$.

steps.title: Heat lost by metal heat gained by water
steps.content: Heat lost by metal = heat gained by water: $0.05\times350\times(T-26)=0.1\times4200\times6$.

question: The variables $x$ and $y$ are related by the equation $x = 5 - \dfrac{2y^3+1}{1-2y^3}$. Which of the following is a rearrangement to make $y$ the subject?
technique: $x=5-\dfrac{2y^3+1}{1-2y^3}$. Multiplying out: $x(1-2y^3)=5(1-2y^3)-(2y^3+1) \Rightarrow x-2xy^3=4-12y^3 \Rightarrow y^3(12-2x)=4-x \Rightarrow y^3=\dfrac{x-4}{2x-12} \Rightarrow y=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{x-4}{2x-12}}$.

steps.title: Simplify to the result
steps.content: Multiplying out: $x(1-2y^3)=5(1-2y^3)-(2y^3+1) \Rightarrow x-2xy^3=4-12y^3 \Rightarrow y^3(12-2x)=4-x \Rightarrow y^3=\dfrac{x-4}{2x-12} \Rightarrow y=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{x-4}{2x-12}}$.

question: A bar magnet is placed at position X close to one end of a coil, on the axis of the coil. [DIAGRAM: coil with bar magnet, velocity-time graph showing the magnet moving from X to Y and back to X]. The graph shows how the velocity of the magnet varies as it moves rapidly to position Y and back to position X. The magnetic field of the bar magnet still affects the coil when at position Y. Which graph represents how the induced voltage in the coil changes as the magnet moves?

options.A: square-wave matching the velocity graph shape exactly (constant positive then constant negative)
options.B: rises from 0 to a peak, dips to 0, rises again to a peak
options.C: single positive rectangular pulse then drops to zero
options.D: starts high positive, decays curving toward zero, small step down to slightly negative, then curves further to more negative by the end
options.E: starts at a peak, descends to a trough near zero, then rises back to a peak (symmetric bowl)
options.F: rises from zero to a positive peak, sharp drop to a negative trough, then curves back toward zero

technique: The magnet's field gradient is steepest close to the coil (position X) and shallow far away (position Y), so even though velocity is a square wave, induced EMF (which depends on rate of flux change, i.e. field gradient x velocity) decays as the magnet moves away from X to Y, then grows in magnitude (more negative) as it approaches X again on the return: starting high positive, decaying curving toward zero, a small step down to slightly negative, then curving further to more negative by the end.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: The magnet s field gradient is steepest close
steps.content: The magnet's field gradient is steepest close to the coil (position X) and shallow far away (position Y), so even though velocity is a square wave, induced EMF (which depends on rate of flux change, i.e. field gradient x velocity) decays as the magnet moves away from X to Y, then grows in magnitude (more negative) as it approaches X again on the return: starting high positive, decaying curving toward zero, a small step down to slightly negative, then curving further to more negative by the end.

subtopic: Probability of forming a right-angled triangle
question: Three different numbers are chosen at random from 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. What is the probability that the three numbers form the three sides of a right-angled triangle?
technique: There are $\binom{5}{3}=10$ equally likely triples from $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$. A triple forms a right-angled triangle exactly when it is a Pythagorean triple, so test each: only $(3,4,5)$ works, since $3^2+4^2=9+16=25=5^2$. That is one favourable outcome out of ten, so the probability is $\dfrac{1}{10}$.

steps.title: There are equally likely triples from
steps.content: There are $\binom{5}{3}=10$ equally likely triples from $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$.

steps.title: A triple forms a right angled triangle exactly
steps.content: A triple forms a right-angled triangle exactly when it is a Pythagorean triple, so test each: only $(3,4,5)$ works, since $3^2+4^2=9+16=25=5^2$.

steps.title: That is one favourable outcome out of ten
steps.content: That is one favourable outcome out of ten, so the probability is $\dfrac{1}{10}$.

subtopic: Spring energy converted to gravitational PE
question: A small slider of mass 30 g is at rest near the bottom of a frictionless slope, in contact with a light uncompressed spring. The spring is compressed by 5.0 cm and the slider remains in contact with it. The spring is released and causes the slider to rise up the slope to a maximum vertical height of 20 cm. The slider is replaced with one of mass 20 g. The spring is now compressed by 15 cm, and the new slider remains in contact with it. To what maximum vertical height does this new slider rise after release? (The spring obeys Hooke's law; assume air resistance is negligible.)
technique: First case: $\tfrac12 k(0.05)^2 = 0.03\times10\times0.20 \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{2\times0.03\times10\times0.20}{0.05^2}=48\,\text{N/m}$. Second case: $E=\tfrac12\times48\times0.15^2=0.54\,\text{J}$. Height $h=\dfrac{0.54}{0.02\times10}=2.7\,\text{m}=270\,\text{cm}$.

question: The point $(-1, 5)$ is translated to the point $(3, 2)$ by two successive translations. The first translation is by the vector $\begin{pmatrix}3p\\-4p\end{pmatrix}$ and the second is by the vector $\begin{pmatrix}q\\-2q\end{pmatrix}$. What is the value of $p+q$?
technique: The total displacement is $(3-(-1), 2-5)=(4,-3)$. So $3p+q=4$ and $-4p-2q=-3$. From the first, $q=4-3p$; substituting: $4p+2(4-3p)=3 \Rightarrow -2p+8=3 \Rightarrow p=2.5$. Then $q=4-7.5=-3.5$, so $p+q=-1$.

subtopic: Ideal gas, pressure and density under compression
question: A tall, smooth cylinder contains air at atmospheric pressure of $1.00\times10^5\,\text{Pa}$. The density of the air in the cylinder is $1.20\,\text{kg/m}^3$. A heavy piston is now placed in the top of the cylinder and allowed to fall slowly downwards, compressing the air until it rests in equilibrium. The mass of the piston is 50.0 kg and its cross-sectional area is $0.0200\,\text{m}^2$. What is the density of the air in the cylinder when the piston rests in equilibrium? (g = 10 N/kg; assume ideal gas, constant temperature.)
technique: Additional pressure from piston: $\Delta P = mg/A = (50\times10)/0.02=25{,}000\,\text{Pa}$. New pressure $=100{,}000+25{,}000=125{,}000\,\text{Pa}$. Since $T$ constant, density $\propto$ pressure (Boyle's law: $\rho_2=\rho_1\times P_2/P_1=1.20\times125000/100000=1.50\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

steps.title: Additional pressure from piston
steps.content: Additional pressure from piston: $\Delta P = mg/A = (50\times10)/0.02=25{,}000\,\text{Pa}$.

steps.title: Since constant density pressure Boyle s law

question: The diagram represents a stationary wave in a medium spanning 150 cm, showing three complete loops (three full waves) along its length. [DIAGRAM: stationary wave pattern over 150 cm]. The transverse waves creating the stationary wave travel at a speed of 300 m/s through the medium. What is the frequency of the transverse waves?
technique: With three full waves (six half-wavelengths) fitting in 150 cm, $\lambda = 150/3 = 50\,\text{cm}=0.5\,\text{m}$. Frequency $f=v/\lambda=300/0.5=600\,\text{Hz}$.

steps.title: With three full waves six half wavelengths fitting
steps.content: With three full waves (six half-wavelengths) fitting in 150 cm, $\lambda = 150/3 = 50\,\text{cm}=0.5\,\text{m}$.

subtopic: Triangle area with vertex on a circle
question: The diagram shows a semicircle of radius 5 units centred at $(5,0)$, spanning from $(0,0)$ to $(10,0)$. A triangle has vertices at $(0,0)$, $(10,0)$ and $(x,y)$, where $(x,y)$ is a point on the arc of the semicircle. Which of the following is an expression in terms of $x$ for the area of this triangle?
technique: Since $(x,y)$ lies on the circle $(x-5)^2+y^2=25$, this gives $y^2 = 25-(x-5)^2=10x-x^2$, so $y=\sqrt{10x-x^2}$. Triangle area $=\tfrac12\times\text{base}\times\text{height}=\tfrac12\times10\times y = 5\sqrt{10x-x^2}$.

steps.title: Since lies on the circle this gives so
steps.content: Since $(x,y)$ lies on the circle $(x-5)^2+y^2=25$, this gives $y^2 = 25-(x-5)^2=10x-x^2$, so $y=\sqrt{10x-x^2}$.

subtopic: Projectile motion, gravitational field strength comparison
question: A stone is thrown vertically upwards from the surface of the Earth and reaches a maximum height $h$. The same stone is thrown vertically upwards from the surface of the Moon, with the same initial speed. What is the maximum height reached on the Moon? (g on Earth = 10 N/kg; g on Moon = 1.6 N/kg; ignore air resistance.)
technique: Using $v^2=2gh \Rightarrow h=v^2/(2g)$, so $h$ is inversely proportional to $g$. Since $v$ is the same, $h_{Moon}=h\times\dfrac{g_{Earth}}{g_{Moon}}=h\times\dfrac{10}{1.6}$.

steps.title: Using so is inversely proportional to
steps.content: Using $v^2=2gh \Rightarrow h=v^2/(2g)$, so $h$ is inversely proportional to $g$.

question: Four mathematically similar solids, W, X, Y and Z, have the following properties: the ratio of the lengths of W to X is 1:2; the ratio of the surface area of X to Y is 2:1; the ratio of the volume of Y to Z is 1:2. What is the order of the solids when arranged in increasing volume?
technique: Length ratio W:X = 1:2 gives volume ratio W:X = $1:8$. Surface area ratio X:Y = 2:1 gives length ratio X:Y = $\sqrt2:1$, so volume ratio X:Y = $2\sqrt2:1$. Setting W=1, X=8, then Y $= 8/(2\sqrt2)\approx2.83$. Volume ratio Y:Z=1:2 gives Z $\approx5.66$. Increasing order: W(1) < Y(2.83) < Z(5.66) < X(8), i.e. W Y Z X.

steps.title: Length ratio W X gives volume ratio W
steps.content: Length ratio W:X = 1:2 gives volume ratio W:X = $1:8$.

steps.title: Surface area ratio X Y gives length ratio
steps.content: Surface area ratio X:Y = 2:1 gives length ratio X:Y = $\sqrt{2}:1$, so volume ratio X:Y = $2\sqrt{2}:1$.

steps.title: Volume ratio Y Z gives Z
Increasing order W Y Z X i e
steps.content: Increasing order: W(1) < Y(2.83) < Z(5.66) < X(8), i.e.

question: A ray of light is incident on a boundary between a vacuum and medium X at angle $\theta$ to the normal. [DIAGRAM: incident ray, reflected ray, and refracted ray at a vacuum-medium X boundary]. The incident ray is partially reflected and partially refracted. The angle between the reflected and refracted rays is 90°. What is the refractive index of medium X?
technique: The reflected ray makes angle $\theta$ with the normal (law of reflection). Since the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular, the refraction angle $r=90°-\theta$. By Snell's law, $n=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\sin r}=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\sin(90°-\theta)}=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}=\tan\theta$.

steps.title: The reflected ray makes angle with the normal
steps.content: The reflected ray makes angle $\theta$ with the normal (law of reflection).

steps.title: Since the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular
steps.content: Since the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular, the refraction angle $r=90^\circ-\theta$.

question: Which one of the following expressions is equal to $\dfrac{(2+\sqrt{20})^2}{(1+\sqrt5)^3}$?
technique: $\sqrt{20}=2\sqrt5$, so $2+\sqrt{20}=2(1+\sqrt5)$, giving $(2+\sqrt{20})^2=4(1+\sqrt5)^2$. So the expression $=\dfrac{4(1+\sqrt5)^2}{(1+\sqrt5)^3}=\dfrac{4}{1+\sqrt5}=\dfrac{4(\sqrt5-1)}{(\sqrt5+1)(\sqrt5-1)}=\dfrac{4(\sqrt5-1)}{4}=\sqrt5-1$.

subtopic: Kinematics, force, and power from displacement-time equation
question: An electric train travels along a straight horizontal track, passing point Q at $t=0$. Its distance from Q is given by $x = at + bt^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants. Which of the following statements is/are correct? 1: The speed of the train increases with time at a constant rate. 2: The resultant force acting on the train increases with time. 3: The rate at which energy is transferred to the train increases with time.
technique: $v=dx/dt=a+2bt$, which increases linearly with time (constant rate) — statement 1 true. Acceleration $=dv/dt=2b$ is constant, so resultant force $F=m(2b)$ is constant, not increasing — statement 2 false. Power $=Fv=2mb(a+2bt)$ increases with time since $v$ increases while $F$ is constant — statement 3 true. So statements 1 and 3 only.

steps.title: Which increases linearly with time constant rate statement
steps.content: $v=dx/dt=a+2bt$, which increases linearly with time (constant rate) — statement 1 true.

steps.title: Acceleration is constant so resultant force is constant
steps.content: Acceleration $=dv/dt=2b$ is constant, so resultant force $F=m(2b)$ is constant, not increasing — statement 2 false.

steps.title: Power increases with time since increases while is
steps.content: Power $=Fv=2mb(a+2bt)$ increases with time since $v$ increases while $F$ is constant — statement 3 true.

question: Evaluate $\displaystyle\sum_{r=2}^{\infty}\left(\dfrac{1}{2^r}+\dfrac{1}{3^r}+\dfrac{1}{4^r}\right)$.
technique: Each is a geometric series starting at $r=2$: $\sum_{r=2}^\infty \frac{1}{k^r} = \dfrac{1/k^2}{1-1/k}$. For $k=2$: $\dfrac{1/4}{1/2}=\dfrac12$. For $k=3$: $\dfrac{1/9}{2/3}=\dfrac16$. For $k=4$: $\dfrac{1/16}{3/4}=\dfrac{1}{12}$. Sum $=\dfrac12+\dfrac16+\dfrac1{12}=\dfrac{6}{12}+\dfrac{2}{12}+\dfrac{1}{12}=\dfrac{9}{12}=\dfrac34$.

steps.title: Each is a geometric series starting at
steps.content: Each is a geometric series starting at $r=2$: $\sum_{r=2}^\infty \frac{1}{k^r} = \dfrac{1/k^2}{1-1/k}$.

subtopic: Resistance and resistivity with dimension scaling, parallel combination
question: A length of wire has resistance $R$. Another length of wire is made from the same material, twice as long, with half the diameter. Both wires have circular cross-sections. The two wires are connected in parallel. What is the total resistance of this combination?
technique: $R=\rho L/A$. The second wire has $L_2=2L$ and $A_2=A/4$ (half diameter $\Rightarrow$ quarter area), so $R_2=\rho(2L)/(A/4)=8\rho L/A=8R$. In parallel: $R_{total}=\dfrac{R\times8R}{R+8R}=\dfrac{8R}{9}$.

steps.title: The second wire has and half diameter quarter
steps.content: The second wire has $L_2=2L$ and $A_2=A/4$ (half diameter $\Rightarrow$ quarter area), so $R_2=\rho(2L)/(A/4)=8\rho L/A=8R$.

question: What is the complete set of real values of $x$ for which $x^2(x^2+4) < 21$?
technique: $x^2(x^2+4)<21 \Rightarrow x^4+4x^2-21<0$. Let $u=x^2$: $u^2+4u-21<0 \Rightarrow (u+7)(u-3)<0 \Rightarrow -7<u<3$. Since $u=x^2\ge0$, this gives $0\le x^2<3$, i.e. $-\sqrt3<x<\sqrt3$.

subtopic: Work, efficiency, lifting water from a well
question: An empty bucket has a mass of 1.20 kg and internal volume of $0.0150\,\text{m}^3$. The bucket is used to lift water from a well, attached to a rope winding onto a rotating cylinder of radius 0.200 m turned by a handle. To lift a bucket completely full of water at constant speed, a force of 250 N must be applied to the handle, acting tangent to the circle of radius 0.600 m in which the handle moves. The energy required to lift the (empty) bucket is wasted energy. What is the efficiency of the system in lifting water from the well? (g = 10 N/kg; density of water = 1000 kg/m³.)
technique: Water mass $=1000\times0.015=15\,\text{kg}$, weight $=150\,\text{N}$; bucket weight $=12\,\text{N}$. Per revolution, input work $\propto 250\times0.6$ and output (useful) work $\propto 150\times0.2$ (same rope displacement factor of $2\pi$ cancels). Efficiency $=\dfrac{150\times0.2}{250\times0.6}=\dfrac{30}{150}=0.20=20.0\%$.

steps.title: Per revolution input work and output useful work
steps.content: Per revolution, input work $\propto 250\times0.6$ and output (useful) work $\propto 150\times0.2$ (same rope displacement factor of $2\pi$ cancels).

question: A sector of a circle has perimeter 24. For what value of the radius does the sector have the maximum possible area?
technique: Perimeter: $2r+r\theta=24 \Rightarrow \theta=\dfrac{24-2r}{r}$. Area $=\tfrac12 r^2\theta=\tfrac12r^2\cdot\dfrac{24-2r}{r}=12r-r^2$. Differentiating: $\dfrac{dA}{dr}=12-2r=0 \Rightarrow r=6$.

question: A uniform metre ruler has a mass of 100 g and is 1.00 m long. A small object of mass 20 g is fixed at the 0 cm mark, and another of mass 80 g at the 100 cm mark. The ruler is balanced on a pivot. When balanced, what distance is the pivot from the 0 cm mark?
technique: Taking moments about the pivot at distance $d$ from the 0 cm mark, with the ruler's own mass acting at 50 cm: $20(0)+100(50)+80(100)=200d \Rightarrow d=\dfrac{5000+8000}{200}=65\,\text{cm}$.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Taking moments about the pivot at distance from
steps.content: Taking moments about the pivot at distance $d$ from the 0 cm mark, with the ruler's own mass acting at 50 cm: $20(0)+100(50)+80(100)=200d \Rightarrow d=\dfrac{5000+8000}{200}=65\,\text{cm}$.

subtopic: Quadratic curve, rectangle from roots and turning point
question: The curve $y=x^2-x-6$ intersects the x-axis at points A and B, and has a minimum at point C. The rectangle ABDE has two of its vertices at A and B, and C lies on edge DE, between D and E. What is the area of the rectangle ABDE?
technique: $y=x^2-x-6=(x-3)(x+2)$, so roots are $x=-2,3$, giving $AB=5$. The minimum is at $x=\tfrac12$: $y=\tfrac14-\tfrac12-6=-6.25$, so the vertical distance from AB to the minimum (and hence to DE) is $6.25$. Rectangle area $=5\times6.25=31.25$.

steps.title: The minimum is at so the vertical distance
steps.content: The minimum is at $x=\tfrac12$: $y=\tfrac14-\tfrac12-6=-6.25$, so the vertical distance from AB to the minimum (and hence to DE) is $6.25$.

subtopic: Kirchhoff's laws, circuit with two batteries
question: A circuit comprising two resistors (3.0 Ω and 1.0 Ω) and two batteries (3.0 V and 6.0 V), with negligible internal resistance, is set up as shown, with junctions X and Y. [DIAGRAM: circuit with 3.0 Ω and 1.0 Ω resistors and 3.0 V, 6.0 V batteries between junctions X and Y]. What are the magnitude and direction of the current in the 3.0 V battery?
technique: Applying Kirchhoff's voltage and current laws to the two-loop circuit with the given branch resistances and EMFs yields a current of 2.0 A in the 3.0 V battery branch, directed from Y to X.

steps.title: Applying Kirchhoff s voltage and current laws to
steps.content: Applying Kirchhoff's voltage and current laws to the two-loop circuit with the given branch resistances and EMFs yields a current of 2.0 A in the 3.0 V battery branch, directed from Y to X.

subtopic: Perpendicular distance from a point to a line
question: A straight line passes through the points $(0, 2a)$ and $(a, 0)$, where $a$ is a positive constant. What is the perpendicular distance of the point $P(a, 2a)$ from this line?
technique: The line through $(0,2a)$ and $(a,0)$ has equation $2x+y-2a=0$ (slope $-2$). Perpendicular distance from $P(a,2a)$: $d=\dfrac{|2(a)+2a-2a|}{\sqrt{4+1}}=\dfrac{2a}{\sqrt5}=\dfrac{2\sqrt5}{5}a$.

steps.title: The line through and has equation slope
steps.content: The line through $(0,2a)$ and $(a,0)$ has equation $2x+y-2a=0$ (slope $-2$).

subtopic: Perfectly inelastic collision, kinetic energy loss
question: Two isolated spheres have masses $m$ and $2m$. They move towards each other along the same line with speeds $4v$ and $v$ respectively, then collide and coalesce. What is the loss of kinetic energy during the collision?
technique: Taking the direction of mass $m$ as positive, momentum conservation: $m(4v)+2m(-v)=3m\,v_f \Rightarrow v_f=\dfrac{2v}{3}$. Initial KE $=\tfrac12m(4v)^2+\tfrac12(2m)v^2=8mv^2+mv^2=9mv^2$. Final KE $=\tfrac12(3m)\left(\dfrac{2v}{3}\right)^2=\dfrac{2}{3}mv^2$. Loss $=9mv^2-\dfrac23mv^2=\dfrac{25}{3}mv^2$.

steps.title: Taking the direction of mass as positive momentum
steps.content: Taking the direction of mass $m$ as positive, momentum conservation: $m(4v)+2m(-v)=3m\,v_f \Rightarrow v_f=\dfrac{2v}{3}$.

question: Find the sum of the real solutions to the equation $2^x - \left(\sqrt2\right)^{x+6} + 12 = 0$.

subtopic: Young modulus, extension of a hanging cable under its own weight
question: A steel cable has mass 64 kg and cross-sectional area $2.0\times10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$. The Young modulus of steel is $2.0\times10^{11}\,\text{Pa}$. When the cable lies on horizontal ground its length is 40 m. What is its extension when it is suspended freely from one end and hangs vertically? (g = 10 N/kg; assume the cable obeys Hooke's law.)
technique: For a cable hanging under its own weight, the tension varies linearly from 0 at the bottom to $W=mg$ at the top, so the effective average force is $W/2$. Extension $=\dfrac{(W/2)L}{AE}=\dfrac{(640/2)\times40}{2.0\times10^{-4}\times2.0\times10^{11}}=\dfrac{12800}{4\times10^7}=3.2\times10^{-4}\,\text{m}$.

steps.title: For a cable hanging under its own weight
steps.content: For a cable hanging under its own weight, the tension varies linearly from 0 at the bottom to $W=mg$ at the top, so the effective average force is $W/2$.
